articles.title: Breakthrough in Diabetic Retinopathy: Protein LRG1 Identified as Early Trigger
articles.summary: Researchers from UCL have identified a protein named LRG1 as a key factor in triggering diabetic retinopathy, a leading cause of vision loss in adults with diabetes. The study, published in Science Translational Medicine and funded by Diabetes UK and other charities, reveals that LRG1 causes cells around the eye's smallest blood vessels to constrict excessively, reducing oxygen flow to the retina and initiating damage that can lead to blindness. Using diabetic mouse models, scientists blocked LRG1 activity and prevented early retinal damage, preserving normal eye function. This suggests that targeting LRG1 could enable therapies to intervene before significant harm occurs, unlike current treatments that focus on the protein VEGF and often start too late. The research highlights the potential for a new drug in development to stop diabetic retinopathy from progressing, offering promise for millions worldwide.

articles.title: Uncovering How Ancient Plague Spread Through Livestock
articles.summary: Scientists have made a breakthrough in understanding the spread of an ancient plague that occurred during the Bronze Age, about 5,000 years ago. Unlike the medieval Black Death, which was transmitted by fleas, this earlier strain of Yersinia pestis lacked flea transmission, puzzling researchers for years. An international team, led by archaeologist Taylor Hermes from the University of Arkansas, discovered Y. pestis DNA in the remains of a domesticated sheep from Arkaim, a fortified settlement in Russia's Southern Ural Mountains. This finding, published in the journal Cell, suggests that livestock such as sheep may have played a key role in spreading the plague across Eurasia. The research involved analyzing ancient DNA from animal bones, which is challenging due to contamination and degradation. By studying these remains, scientists can trace the movement of domesticated animals and uncover pathogens that affected both herds and humans. This discovery provides insights into how diseases evolved and spread in prehistoric times, highlighting the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration in archaeology and genetics.

articles.title: Unraveling Antarctica's Gravity Anomaly: Deep Earth Processes Revealed
articles.summary: Scientists have identified a gravity anomaly beneath Antarctica, where gravitational pull is weaker than elsewhere on Earth. This phenomenon, known as the Antarctic gravity hole, results from variations in rock density deep within the planet's interior, caused by slow geological movements over tens of millions of years. Researchers Alessandro Forte and Petar Glišović used earthquake data and computer models to map this anomaly, comparing their results with satellite measurements for accuracy. Their study, published in Scientific Reports, reconstructs the gravity hole's evolution, showing it strengthened between 50 and 30 million years ago, coinciding with Antarctica's glaciation. The weaker gravity affects sea levels, causing lower ocean surfaces around the continent. This research aims to link Earth's internal processes, such as mantle dynamics, to surface climate changes, potentially explaining ice sheet development. Future work will explore interactions between gravity, sea level, and elevation to better understand planetary systems.

articles.title: Physicists Confirm 50-Year-Old Theory with Magnetic Vortices in Ultrathin Materials
articles.summary: Physicists have experimentally confirmed a theoretical model from the 1970s by observing magnetic vortices in an ultrathin material. In a study published in Nature Materials, researchers led by The University of Texas at Austin cooled nickel phosphorus trisulfide (NiPS3) to temperatures between -150 and -130 °C, inducing a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless (BKT) phase. In this phase, magnetic moments form vortices—swirling structures that pair in opposite directions. As temperatures dropped further, the material transitioned to a six-state clock ordered phase, where magnetic moments align in one of six symmetric directions. This sequence validates the two-dimensional six-state clock model, predicting such phases. The discovery, first proposed decades ago, could advance nanoscale technologies by enabling control of magnetism at tiny scales. Researchers aim to stabilize these phases at higher temperatures for practical applications, suggesting other 2D materials may host similar unknown phases, opening avenues for new physics and device concepts.

articles.title: Forensic Moss Evidence in a Cemetery Grave Robbing Case
articles.summary: In 2009, employees at Burr Oak Cemetery in Alsip, Chicago, were discovered exhuming old graves to resell burial plots, dumping remains elsewhere on the grounds. This grave-robbing scandal involved desecrating hundreds of graves, including that of Emmett Till, with skeletal remains found in piles. The perpetrators were convicted in 2015, aided by forensic evidence from moss. A new paper in Forensic Sciences Research details how moss helped establish a timeline for the reburial of remains. The FBI collected moss samples buried with the bones and consulted Matt von Konrat, a botany expert at the Field Museum, to identify the species and determine how long it had been buried. Mosses, as bryophyte plants, preserve timeline and habitat information due to their resilient nature and metabolic activity, even when dry. Chlorophyll degradation in moss allowed scientists to measure light absorption and compare it to control specimens, providing crucial evidence. This case highlights the underutilized role of botanical evidence in forensics and aims to raise awareness among investigators. Burr Oak Cemetery, founded in 1927 for Chicago's African American community, houses notable figures, adding historical significance to the crime.

articles.title: The Evolution of Vertebrate Eyes: A Cyclopean Origin
articles.summary: A recent study published in Current Biology proposes a novel hypothesis for the evolution of vertebrate eyes. Researchers from the University of Sussex and Lund University suggest that vertebrate eyes, including human eyes, may not have descended directly from the paired eyes of early bilaterian animals. Instead, they argue that after the lineage leading to vertebrates split from other bilaterians, our ancestors adopted a burrowing lifestyle, making paired eyes unnecessary and costly to maintain, leading to their loss. Later, when some descendants returned to free-swimming, they needed eyes for navigation but lacked the original rhabdomeric-based eyes. The hypothesis posits that they 'reinvented' eyes from a single, centrally located light-sensitive organ, called a cyclopean organ, which monitored ambient light. This process resulted in vertebrate eyes using ciliary photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) for vision, unlike most invertebrates that use rhabdomeric cells. This explains key differences in eye structure and function between vertebrates and other animal groups, such as arthropods and cephalopods, offering insights into evolutionary adaptation and neural development.
